questions: You're someone who prioritizes the team over your own position and readily accepts change. Your manager mentions the company is reorganizing and some roles may be consolidated. What do you do?

You're someone who prioritizes the team over your own position and readily accepts change. A colleague tells you that a new hire has been asked to shadow your role for a few weeks. How do you respond?
You're someone who prioritizes the team over your own position and readily accepts change. You hear that a project you've been leading might be reassigned to another department. What's your reaction?
You're someone who prioritizes the team over your own position and readily accepts change. Your boss asks you to document all your processes so anyone could take over. What do you do?

You're someone who prioritizes the team over your own position and readily accepts change. At a team meeting, someone suggests automating a task that currently only you know how to do. How do you react?
You're someone who prioritizes the team over your own position and readily accepts change. You find out your contract might not be renewed next quarter. What steps do you take?
You're someone who prioritizes the team over your own position and readily accepts change. A friend at work warns you that your department might be merged with another one. What do you think about?

You're someone who prioritizes the team over your own position and readily accepts change. Your annual review reveals that a junior team member has been rated higher than you. How do you process this?

You're someone who prioritizes the team over your own position and readily accepts change. The board announces budget cuts and asks each department to justify its existence. How do you prepare your presentation?

You're someone who prioritizes the team over your own position and readily accepts change. You learn that a consulting firm has been hired to evaluate whether your function should be outsourced. What do you do?
